---
title: "Background"
format:
  html:
    page-layout: full
---

## Hantavirus background table

<div class="hanta-table-wrapper">

<table class="hanta-table">
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th>Virus</th>
      <th>Geographic region</th>
      <th>Human disease</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>

  <tbody>
    <tr class="row-green">
      <td colspan="3"><strong>NEW WORLD HANTAVIRUSES (Americas) causing Cardiopulmonary or pulmonary syndrome (HCPS/HPS)</strong></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>Sin Nombre virus (SNV) – main cause in U.S./Canada</td>
      <td>North America</td>
      <td>HCPS</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>Black Creek Canal virus</td>
      <td>North America</td>
      <td>HCPS</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>Bayou virus</td>
      <td>North America</td>
      <td>HCPS</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>Monongahela virus</td>
      <td>North America</td>
      <td>HCPS</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>Andes virus (ANDV) – main cause / notable for limited human-to-human transmission</td>
      <td>South America</td>
      <td>HCPS</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>Araraquara virus</td>
      <td>South America</td>
      <td>HCPS</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>Laguna Negra virus</td>
      <td>South America</td>
      <td>HCPS</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>Juquitiba virus</td>
      <td>South America</td>
      <td>HCPS</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>Bermejo virus</td>
      <td>South America</td>
      <td>HCPS</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>Lechiguanas virus</td>
      <td>South America</td>
      <td>HCPS</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>Maciel virus</td>
      <td>South America</td>
      <td>HCPS</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>Orán virus</td>
      <td>South America</td>
      <td>HCPS</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>Choclo virus</td>
      <td>Central America</td>
      <td>HCPS</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>Tula virus (TULV)</td>
      <td>Europe/Asia</td>
      <td>Usually mild or subclinical infections</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>New York virus</td>
      <td>North America</td>
      <td>Associated with HPS in northeastern U.S.</td>
    </tr>

    <tr class="row-purple">
      <td colspan="3"><strong> Excluded: OLD WORLD HANTAVIRUSES (Europe/Asia) causing Hemorrhagic Fever with Renal Syndrome (HFRS)</strong></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>Hantaan virus (HTNV)</td>
      <td>East Asia (China, Korea)</td>
      <td>Severe HFRS</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>Dobrava-Belgrade virus (DOBV)</td>
      <td>Balkans / Eastern Europe</td>
      <td>Severe HFRS</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>Seoul virus (SEOV)</td>
      <td>Worldwide (rats)</td>
      <td>Moderate HFRS</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>Puumala virus (PUUV)</td>
      <td>Northern Europe</td>
      <td>Mild HFRS / nephropathia epidemica</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>Saaremaa virus</td>
      <td>Europe</td>
      <td>Mild HFRS</td>
    </tr>
    <tr class="section-divider">
      <td>Amur virus</td>
      <td>Asia</td>
      <td>HFRS</td>
    </tr>

    <tr class="note-row">
      <td colspan="3">
        <strong>Definition:</strong> Hemorrhagic fever refers to bleeding tendencies due to vascular damage. Renal syndrome refers to kidney involvement, often leading to acute kidney injury.
      </td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

</div>


## Old World vs New World Hantaviruses

<div class="hanta-table-wrapper">

<table class="hanta-table comparison-table">
  <thead>
    <tr class="comparison-title">
      <th colspan="4">Old World vs New World Hantaviruses (with reservoirs & hotspots)</th>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <th>Feature</th>
      <th>Old World hantaviruses</th>
      <th>New World hantaviruses</th>
      <th>Reservoirs & geographic hotspots</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>

  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>Geographic distribution</strong></td>
      <td>Europe and Asia</td>
      <td>North, Central, South America</td>
      <td>Old World: Eurasia; New World: Americas</td>
    </tr>

    <tr>
      <td><strong>Main clinical syndrome</strong></td>
      <td>HFRS (Hemorrhagic Fever with Renal Syndrome)</td>
      <td>HCPS/HPS (Hantavirus Cardiopulmonary/Pulmonary Syndrome)</td>
      <td>—</td>
    </tr>

    <tr>
      <td><strong>Primary organs affected</strong></td>
      <td>Kidneys, vascular system</td>
      <td>Lungs and heart</td>
      <td>—</td>
    </tr>

    <tr>
      <td><strong>Case fatality rate</strong></td>
      <td>~&lt;1% to ~15%</td>
      <td>Up to ~40–50%</td>
      <td>—</td>
    </tr>

    <tr>
      <td><strong>Geographic burden</strong></td>
      <td>~10,000–100,000 cases/year globally</td>
      <td>Much rarer (hundreds–thousands reported)</td>
      <td>—</td>
    </tr>

    <tr>
      <td><strong>Most important viruses</strong></td>
      <td>Hantaan (severe), Puumala (mild), Seoul (moderate)</td>
      <td>Sin Nombre (North America), Andes (South America)</td>
      <td>—</td>
    </tr>

    <tr>
      <td><strong>Rodent reservoir types</strong></td>
      <td>Murinae rodents (rats, Old World mice, voles)</td>
      <td>Sigmodontine rodents (New World mice/rats)</td>
      <td>Each virus linked to a specific rodent host</td>
    </tr>

    <tr>
      <td><strong>Typical reservoirs (examples)</strong></td>
      <td>
        - Hantaan → striped field mouse<br>
        - Puumala → bank vole<br>
        - Seoul → Norway rat
      </td>
      <td>
        - Sin Nombre → deer mouse<br>
        - Andes → long-tailed pygmy rice rat<br>
        - Bayou → rice rat
      </td>
      <td>Strong virus–host specificity</td>
    </tr>

    <tr>
      <td><strong>Hotspot regions</strong></td>
      <td>
        - China, Korea (Hantaan)<br>
        - Northern Europe (Puumala)<br>
        - Balkans (Dobrava)<br>
        - Worldwide urban (Seoul)
      </td>
      <td>
        - U.S. Southwest & Canada (Sin Nombre)<br>
        - Southern South America (Andes)<br>
        - Brazil, Argentina, Paraguay (multiple species)<br>
        - Panama (Choclo)
      </td>
      <td>Geographic range mirrors rodent distribution</td>
    </tr>

    <tr>
      <td><strong>Human-to-human transmission</strong></td>
      <td>None documented</td>
      <td>Rare (Andes virus only)</td>
      <td>Occurs mainly in Argentina/Chile</td>
    </tr>

    <tr>
      <td><strong>Typical exposure setting</strong></td>
      <td>Rural/farming, forest exposure, rodent-infested buildings</td>
      <td>Rural, peridomestic, occupational exposure</td>
      <td>Aerosolized rodent excreta is main route</td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

</div>
